name: Electrochemistry and electrical circuits and their elements

nodes.label: Electric Charge and Current
nodes.summary: Charge is the fundamental quantity carried by particles, and current is its flow rate through a conductor.

nodes.label: Electric Charge
nodes.summary: Charge is a conserved, quantized property of matter measured in coulombs.

nodes.label: Elementary charge
nodes.summary: The elementary charge e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C is the magnitude of charge on a proton or electron.

nodes.label: Charge quantization
nodes.summary: Any observable charge is an integer multiple of the elementary charge e (Q = ne).

nodes.label: Conservation of charge
nodes.summary: Total electric charge in an isolated system is conserved and never created or destroyed.

nodes.label: The coulomb
nodes.summary: One coulomb is the SI unit of charge, equal to the charge of about 6.24 x 10^18 elementary charges.

nodes.label: Two types of charge and like/unlike interaction
nodes.summary: Charge is positive or negative; like charges repel and unlike charges attract.

nodes.label: Electric Current
nodes.summary: Current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a cross-section of conductor.

nodes.label: Definition of current (I = Q/t)
nodes.summary: Current I = Q/t is the charge passing a point per unit time, measured in amperes (C/s).

nodes.label: The ampere
nodes.summary: One ampere equals one coulomb of charge flowing per second.

nodes.label: Conventional current direction
nodes.summary: Conventional current flows in the direction of positive charge movement, opposite to electron flow.

nodes.label: Direct current vs alternating current
nodes.summary: Direct current flows steadily in one direction while alternating current periodically reverses direction.

nodes.label: Drift velocity
nodes.summary: Drift velocity is the slow average net velocity of charge carriers superimposed on their random thermal motion (I = nqvA).

nodes.label: Charge carriers
nodes.summary: Charge carriers are mobile charges (electrons in metals, ions in solutions) responsible for current.

nodes.label: Conductors, insulators, and semiconductors
nodes.summary: Conductors have abundant mobile charges, insulators have almost none, and semiconductors are intermediate and tunable.

nodes.label: Resistance and Ohm's Law
nodes.summary: Ohm's law relates voltage, current, and resistance, the property opposing charge flow.

nodes.label: Voltage / Potential Difference
nodes.summary: Voltage is the work per unit charge needed to move charge between two points.

nodes.label: Voltage as potential difference
nodes.summary: Voltage V = W/q is the electric potential energy difference per unit charge between two points, in volts.

nodes.label: The volt
nodes.summary: One volt is one joule of energy per coulomb of charge.

nodes.label: Electromotive force (emf)
nodes.summary: Emf is the energy per unit charge supplied by a source such as a battery, driving current around a circuit.

nodes.label: Terminal voltage and internal resistance
nodes.summary: Terminal voltage V = emf - Ir is less than emf because of voltage dropped across the battery's internal resistance r.

nodes.label: Grounding and reference potential
nodes.summary: Ground is an arbitrary reference point assigned zero potential against which voltages are measured.

nodes.label: Resistance
nodes.summary: Resistance quantifies how strongly a component opposes current flow.

nodes.label: Ohm's law (V = IR)
nodes.summary: Ohm's law states V = IR, the voltage across an ohmic resistor is proportional to the current.

nodes.label: The ohm
nodes.summary: Resistance is measured in ohms, where one ohm is one volt per ampere.

nodes.label: Ohmic vs non-ohmic materials
nodes.summary: Ohmic materials have constant resistance (linear V-I), while non-ohmic devices like diodes do not.

nodes.label: Resistivity and the geometry formula (R = rho L / A)
nodes.summary: Resistance R = rho L/A increases with length and resistivity and decreases with cross-sectional area.

nodes.label: Resistivity as a material property
nodes.summary: Resistivity rho is an intrinsic material property; conductors have low rho and insulators have high rho.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence of resistance
nodes.summary: Resistivity of metallic conductors rises with temperature while that of semiconductors falls.

nodes.label: Conductance and conductivity
nodes.summary: Conductance is the reciprocal of resistance and conductivity is the reciprocal of resistivity.

nodes.label: Electric Power and Energy
nodes.summary: Electric power is the rate at which a circuit element converts electrical energy.

nodes.label: Power Equations
nodes.summary: Power dissipated or delivered can be computed from voltage, current, and resistance.

nodes.label: Power formula (P = IV)
nodes.summary: Electric power P = IV is the rate of energy transfer by a circuit element, in watts.

nodes.label: Joule heating (P = I^2 R)
nodes.summary: Power dissipated as heat in a resistor is P = I^2 R, the basis of Joule heating.

nodes.label: Power form P = V^2 / R
nodes.summary: Power in a resistor can also be written P = V^2/R using Ohm's law substitution.

nodes.label: Maximum power transfer
nodes.summary: A source delivers maximum power to a load when the load resistance equals the source's internal resistance.

nodes.label: Electrical Energy
nodes.summary: Electrical energy is power integrated over time.

nodes.label: Electrical energy (E = Pt)
nodes.summary: Electrical energy consumed is E = Pt = IVt, with the kilowatt-hour as a common practical unit.

nodes.label: The watt
nodes.summary: One watt equals one joule per second of energy transfer.

nodes.label: RMS voltage and current
nodes.summary: For AC, the rms value equals peak/sqrt(2) and gives the same average power as an equivalent DC value.

nodes.label: DC Circuits and Circuit Analysis
nodes.summary: Circuit analysis uses series/parallel rules and conservation laws to find currents and voltages.

nodes.label: Circuit Elements and Diagrams
nodes.summary: Circuits are built from sources and passive elements represented by standard symbols.

nodes.label: Battery / voltage source
nodes.summary: A battery is a source of emf that drives current through a circuit.

nodes.label: Resistor
nodes.summary: A resistor is a circuit element that dissipates energy and limits current via its resistance.

nodes.label: Capacitor as a circuit element
nodes.summary: A capacitor stores charge and blocks steady DC current once fully charged, acting as an open circuit at steady state.

nodes.label: Wires and switches
nodes.summary: Ideal wires have negligible resistance and a switch opens or closes a conducting path.

nodes.label: Open circuits and short circuits
nodes.summary: An open circuit breaks the path so no current flows, while a short circuit provides a near-zero-resistance bypass with large current.

nodes.label: Ammeters and voltmeters
nodes.summary: An ideal ammeter (zero resistance) is placed in series and an ideal voltmeter (infinite resistance) in parallel.

nodes.label: Series and Parallel Combinations
nodes.summary: Resistors and other elements combine differently depending on series or parallel arrangement.

nodes.label: Resistors in series
nodes.summary: Series resistances add directly: R_eq = R1 + R2 + ... and the same current flows through each.

nodes.label: Resistors in parallel
nodes.summary: Parallel resistances combine reciprocally: 1/R_eq = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... and share the same voltage.

nodes.label: Series current is constant
nodes.summary: In a series circuit the current is identical through every element.

nodes.label: Parallel voltage is constant
nodes.summary: In a parallel circuit each branch experiences the same voltage across it.

nodes.label: Equivalent resistance reduction
nodes.summary: Complex networks are simplified by stepwise reduction of series and parallel sub-networks into one equivalent resistance.

nodes.label: Parallel equivalent is smaller than any branch
nodes.summary: Adding resistors in parallel lowers total resistance below that of the smallest branch and raises total current.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's Laws
nodes.summary: Kirchhoff's laws apply conservation of charge and energy to analyze any circuit.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's junction (current) rule
nodes.summary: The sum of currents into a node equals the sum out, expressing conservation of charge.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's loop (voltage) rule
nodes.summary: The sum of voltage changes around any closed loop is zero, expressing conservation of energy.

nodes.label: Voltage divider
nodes.summary: Series resistors split the source voltage in proportion to their resistances.

nodes.label: Current divider
nodes.summary: Parallel resistors split current in inverse proportion to their resistances.

nodes.label: Capacitance and Dielectrics
nodes.summary: Capacitors store charge and electrical energy in an electric field between conductors.

nodes.label: Capacitor Basics
nodes.summary: A capacitor stores charge proportional to the applied voltage.

nodes.label: Capacitance (C = Q/V)
nodes.summary: Capacitance C = Q/V is the charge stored per unit voltage, measured in farads.

nodes.label: The farad
nodes.summary: One farad is one coulomb of stored charge per volt of applied potential difference.

nodes.label: Parallel-plate capacitor (C = epsilon0 A/d)
nodes.summary: For a parallel-plate capacitor C = epsilon0 A/d, increasing with plate area and decreasing with gap.

nodes.label: Uniform field between plates (E = V/d)
nodes.summary: The electric field between parallel plates is uniform with magnitude E = V/d.

nodes.label: Energy Stored in Capacitors
nodes.summary: A charged capacitor stores potential energy in its electric field.

nodes.label: Capacitor energy (U = 1/2 CV^2)
nodes.summary: Energy stored in a capacitor is U = 1/2 CV^2 = 1/2 QV = Q^2/(2C).

nodes.label: Energy density of the electric field
nodes.summary: Energy stored in a capacitor resides in the electric field, with energy density u = 1/2 epsilon0 E^2.

nodes.label: Dielectrics
nodes.summary: Inserting a dielectric increases capacitance by reducing the internal field.

nodes.label: Dielectric constant (C = kappa C0)
nodes.summary: A dielectric of constant kappa multiplies capacitance, C = kappa C0, by polarizing and weakening the field.

nodes.label: Dielectric polarization
nodes.summary: An applied field polarizes a dielectric, creating an opposing field that lowers the net field and net voltage.

nodes.label: Dielectric effect: battery-connected vs isolated
nodes.summary: Inserting a dielectric holds V fixed and raises Q if the battery stays connected, but holds Q fixed and lowers V if the capacitor is isolated.

nodes.label: Dielectric breakdown
nodes.summary: Beyond a critical field strength a dielectric ionizes and conducts, causing dielectric breakdown.

nodes.label: Capacitors in Combination
nodes.summary: Capacitors combine oppositely to resistors in series and parallel.

nodes.label: Capacitors in parallel
nodes.summary: Parallel capacitances add directly: C_eq = C1 + C2 + ... and each shares the same voltage.

nodes.label: Capacitors in series
nodes.summary: Series capacitances combine reciprocally: 1/C_eq = 1/C1 + 1/C2 + ... and each holds the same charge.

nodes.label: RC Circuits
nodes.summary: RC circuits exhibit exponential charging and discharging governed by the time constant.

nodes.label: Charging and Discharging
nodes.summary: Charge on a capacitor changes exponentially when connected through a resistor.

nodes.label: Time constant (tau = RC)
nodes.summary: The RC time constant tau = RC sets the timescale over which a capacitor charges or discharges.

nodes.label: Exponential charging
nodes.summary: During charging the capacitor voltage rises as V = V0(1 - e^(-t/RC)).

nodes.label: Exponential discharging
nodes.summary: During discharging the capacitor voltage decays as V = V0 e^(-t/RC).

nodes.label: Capacitor at the instant of switching
nodes.summary: At t = 0 an uncharged capacitor behaves like a wire (max current), so its initial response mimics a short circuit.

nodes.label: Capacitor in steady state
nodes.summary: At steady state a fully charged capacitor carries no current and acts as an open circuit.

nodes.label: Magnetism and Magnetic Forces
nodes.summary: Moving charges and currents produce and experience magnetic forces and fields.

nodes.label: Magnetic Fields
nodes.summary: Magnetic fields arise from moving charges and have direction and magnitude in teslas.

nodes.label: Source of magnetic fields
nodes.summary: Magnetic fields are produced by moving electric charges and electric currents.

nodes.label: Magnetic field units (tesla, gauss)
nodes.summary: Magnetic field strength B is measured in teslas (SI) or gauss, with 1 T = 10^4 G.

nodes.label: Magnetic dipoles and field lines
nodes.summary: Magnets are dipoles with north and south poles; field lines run from north to south outside and form closed loops with no magnetic monopoles.

nodes.label: Magnetic materials (dia-, para-, ferromagnetic)
nodes.summary: Materials are diamagnetic, paramagnetic, or ferromagnetic depending on how they respond to applied fields.

nodes.label: Force on Moving Charges
nodes.summary: A magnetic field exerts a velocity-dependent force on moving charges.

nodes.label: Magnetic force on a charge (F = qvB sin theta)
nodes.summary: A charge moving in a magnetic field feels force F = qvB sin theta, maximal when v is perpendicular to B.

nodes.label: Right-hand rule
nodes.summary: The right-hand rule gives the direction of the magnetic force on a positive charge from v cross B.

nodes.label: Magnetic force does no work
nodes.summary: Because it is perpendicular to velocity, the magnetic force does no work and changes only direction.

nodes.label: Circular motion in a magnetic field
nodes.summary: A charge moving perpendicular to a uniform B field undergoes uniform circular motion with r = mv/(qB).

nodes.label: Force on Current-Carrying Wires
nodes.summary: A current in a magnetic field experiences a force, the basis of motors.

nodes.label: Force on a current-carrying wire (F = BIL sin theta)
nodes.summary: A current-carrying wire in a field feels force F = BIL sin theta.

nodes.label: Magnetic field of a long straight wire
nodes.summary: A long straight current produces a circular magnetic field B = mu0 I/(2 pi r) around it.

nodes.label: Magnetic field of a solenoid
nodes.summary: A solenoid carrying current produces a nearly uniform field inside, the basis of electromagnets, B = mu0 n I.

nodes.label: Force between parallel currents
nodes.summary: Parallel currents in the same direction attract and antiparallel currents repel.

nodes.label: Electrochemistry Fundamentals
nodes.summary: Electrochemistry links redox reactions to electrical energy through oxidation and reduction at electrodes.

nodes.label: Redox Foundations
nodes.summary: Electrochemical cells are driven by coupled oxidation and reduction half-reactions.

nodes.label: Oxidation and reduction
nodes.summary: Oxidation is loss of electrons and reduction is gain of electrons (OIL RIG).

nodes.label: Oxidation numbers
nodes.summary: Oxidation numbers track electron ownership and identify which species is oxidized or reduced.

nodes.label: Oxidizing and reducing agents
nodes.summary: The oxidizing agent is reduced and the reducing agent is oxidized in a redox reaction.

nodes.label: Half-reactions
nodes.summary: A redox reaction splits into an oxidation half-reaction and a reduction half-reaction.

nodes.label: Balancing redox reactions
nodes.summary: Redox equations are balanced by half-reactions, equalizing atoms, charge, and electrons transferred.

nodes.label: Disproportionation reactions
nodes.summary: In disproportionation a single species is simultaneously oxidized and reduced.

nodes.label: Electrodes and Cell Components
nodes.summary: Every electrochemical cell has an anode, a cathode, an electrolyte, and an external circuit.

nodes.label: Anode (site of oxidation)
nodes.summary: The anode is the electrode where oxidation occurs in any electrochemical cell.

nodes.label: Cathode (site of reduction)
nodes.summary: The cathode is the electrode where reduction occurs in any electrochemical cell.

nodes.label: Electron flow direction
nodes.summary: Electrons always travel from anode to cathode through the external wire in any cell.

nodes.label: Ion flow within the cell
nodes.summary: In the electrolyte cations migrate toward the cathode and anions toward the anode to maintain neutrality.

nodes.label: Electrolyte
nodes.summary: The electrolyte is an ion-conducting medium that completes the internal circuit between electrodes.

nodes.label: Inert vs active electrodes
nodes.summary: Active electrodes participate in the cell reaction while inert electrodes (e.g. Pt, graphite) only provide a surface for electron transfer.

nodes.label: Galvanic (Voltaic) Cells
nodes.summary: Galvanic cells convert spontaneous redox reactions into electrical energy.

nodes.label: Cell Structure and Operation
nodes.summary: A galvanic cell uses two half-cells connected by a salt bridge and an external wire.

nodes.label: Spontaneous operation
nodes.summary: A galvanic cell runs a spontaneous redox reaction (delta G < 0) to generate current.

nodes.label: Salt bridge
nodes.summary: The salt bridge supplies ions to maintain electrical neutrality and completes the circuit between half-cells.

nodes.label: Electrode polarity in galvanic cells
nodes.summary: In a galvanic cell the anode is negative and the cathode is positive.

nodes.label: Electrode mass changes during operation
nodes.summary: The anode loses mass as it is oxidized while the cathode gains mass as metal ions are reduced onto it.

nodes.label: Daniell cell example
nodes.summary: The Daniell cell uses a Zn anode and Cu cathode, with Zn oxidized and Cu2+ reduced.

nodes.label: Cell (line) notation
nodes.summary: Cell notation writes anode | anode solution || cathode solution | cathode, with || the salt bridge.

nodes.label: Concentration cell
nodes.summary: A concentration cell generates voltage from a concentration difference of the same species at two electrodes.

nodes.label: Electrolytic Cells
nodes.summary: Electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous redox reactions.

nodes.label: Electrolysis and Operation
nodes.summary: An external power source forces a nonspontaneous reaction in an electrolytic cell.

nodes.label: Nonspontaneous operation
nodes.summary: An electrolytic cell drives a nonspontaneous reaction (delta G > 0, E_cell < 0) using external energy.

nodes.label: Electrode polarity in electrolytic cells
nodes.summary: In an electrolytic cell the anode is positive and the cathode is negative, reversed from galvanic cells.

nodes.label: Electrolysis
nodes.summary: Electrolysis uses current to decompose a compound, such as splitting water or molten salts.

nodes.label: Competing electrode reactions in aqueous electrolysis
nodes.summary: In aqueous solution water can be oxidized or reduced in competition with dissolved ions, set by reduction potentials and overpotential.

nodes.label: Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis
nodes.summary: Faraday's laws relate the amount of substance produced to the charge passed.

nodes.label: Faraday's constant
nodes.summary: Faraday's constant F = 96485 C/mol is the charge carried by one mole of electrons.

nodes.label: Faraday's law (mass from charge)
nodes.summary: The moles of substance deposited equal charge passed divided by (n x F), linking current and time to mass.

nodes.label: Electroplating and refining
nodes.summary: Electrolysis is applied in electroplating, metal refining, and electrolytic production of elements.

nodes.label: Cell Potential and Thermodynamics
nodes.summary: Cell potential quantifies the driving force of a redox reaction and connects to free energy and equilibrium.

nodes.label: Standard Reduction Potentials
nodes.summary: Standard reduction potentials rank species by tendency to be reduced under standard conditions.

nodes.label: Standard reduction potential
nodes.summary: A standard reduction potential measures a half-reaction's tendency to gain electrons relative to the SHE.

nodes.label: Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)
nodes.summary: The standard hydrogen electrode is the reference assigned a potential of exactly 0 V.

nodes.label: Standard conditions for electrochemistry
nodes.summary: Standard electrochemical conditions are 1 M solute concentrations, 1 atm gas pressures, and usually 25 C.

nodes.label: Standard cell potential (E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode)
nodes.summary: Standard cell potential E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode using both as reduction potentials.

nodes.label: Sign of E_cell and spontaneity
nodes.summary: A positive E_cell indicates a spontaneous (galvanic) reaction and a negative E_cell a nonspontaneous (electrolytic) one.

nodes.label: Cell potential is intensive
nodes.summary: Cell potential does not change when half-reaction coefficients are multiplied, since it is an intensive property.

nodes.label: Activity series and predicting reactions
nodes.summary: The activity series ranks metals by ease of oxidation, predicting which metal displaces another from solution.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic Relationships
nodes.summary: Cell potential is connected to Gibbs free energy and the equilibrium constant.

nodes.label: Free energy and cell potential (delta G = -nFE)
nodes.summary: Gibbs free energy relates to cell potential by delta G = -nFE_cell.

nodes.label: Cell potential and K (delta G = -RT ln K)
nodes.summary: Standard cell potential relates to the equilibrium constant via delta G = -RT ln K = -nFE.

nodes.label: The Nernst Equation
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation gives cell potential under nonstandard concentrations.

nodes.label: Nernst equation
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation E = E0 - (RT/nF) ln Q corrects cell potential for nonstandard concentrations.

nodes.label: Concentration effects on potential
nodes.summary: Changing reactant or product concentrations shifts cell potential via the reaction quotient Q.

nodes.label: E = 0 at equilibrium
nodes.summary: A cell reaches equilibrium (a dead battery) when Q = K and the cell potential equals zero.

nodes.label: Batteries and Practical Cells
nodes.summary: Real-world cells store and deliver energy through redox chemistry in various designs.

nodes.label: Battery Types
nodes.summary: Batteries are classified by whether their reactions can be electrically reversed.

nodes.label: Primary (non-rechargeable) cells
nodes.summary: Primary cells like the alkaline battery use an irreversible reaction and cannot be recharged.

nodes.label: Secondary (rechargeable) cells
nodes.summary: Secondary cells like lead-acid and lithium-ion are recharged by reversing the cell reaction electrolytically.

nodes.label: Recharging reverses the cell to electrolytic mode
nodes.summary: Recharging a secondary cell drives the reaction backward, making it temporarily operate as an electrolytic cell.

nodes.label: Fuel cells
nodes.summary: A fuel cell continuously generates electricity from a fuel such as hydrogen oxidized at the anode.

nodes.label: Corrosion
nodes.summary: Corrosion is an unwanted electrochemical oxidation of metals.

nodes.label: Corrosion as electrochemistry
nodes.summary: Corrosion (e.g. rusting of iron) is the spontaneous electrochemical oxidation of a metal.

nodes.label: Cathodic protection
nodes.summary: Cathodic protection uses a more easily oxidized sacrificial anode to prevent corrosion of the protected metal.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Electric potential energy and electric potential
Coulomb's law and the electrostatic force between charges
Electric field of point charges and Coulomb's law
Vectors and the vector cross product
Centripetal force and uniform circular motion (F = mv^2/r)
Gibbs free energy and reaction spontaneity (delta G)
Equilibrium constant K and the reaction quotient Q
Reaction quotient Q and Le Chatelier's principle
Assigning oxidation states from electronegativity and bonding
Sinusoidal functions and averaging over a period
